entries.text: Railway tracks rise up from the unnatural dirt beside your road. An infernal engine, twisted from a million trapped souls, thunders across the landscape in roughly the same direction as your party. A cabal of warlocks gathers on an open-air carriage somewhere in the middle, conducting a symphony of sorcery. Flames belch from its wheels, and the howl of DragonHearts sends shivers through the haunted ceramics of the HELLPIERCERS armor.

A storm in Hell isn’t just rain and wind. It's made of the same stuff everything else is: dead people. Souls whip themselves into hurricanes and thrash in the thousands against the dirt. They bellow into the air and launch themselves at the sky in a desperate rage. They lash with teeth of red thunder and crush with strange pointed digits emerging from thick dark clouds. The ground is part of it, made of the same soulstuff—it roils like the ocean, swallows like the void. There is a storm coming.

Against the horizon two forms solidify. A titanic serpent and auroch are locked in mortal conflict. The serpent sinks its fangs into the auroch, then swallows the creature whole. The auroch bursts forth from the belly of the serpent in a rain of demonic viscera. As the last piece of gore settles on the ground, it begins to flow back into the serpent’s wound, reinvigorating it. The creatures make to resume their battle, but then their eyes settle onto your convoy.

The Graveyard of Blades stretches out for as far as your eye can see; this is the Hell of weapons, the place spears and bows went after the gun, the place the bullet went after humanity learned to throw stars at each other. Every weapon ever made rests here, along with the spirits of those who could not give them up.

Makeshift towers and the skulls of slain warlords mark this rudimentary highway. A tattered flag warns that you are entering a Psychic-Liberation Zone. These humans reject metanoia, yet still fight against Hell here in its warhold. More and more of these are being reported after the fall of DIS. The humans here despise the pneumatic, and will not treat you with hospitality.

A young girl approaches you from the road. Her hands are cupped, holding pungent and rich soil. She gestures at you pleadingly, thrusting her cupped hands toward you before throwing the soil to the ground. She opens her mouth to speak out her request and in the place of words, more loamy earth spills forward. Soil has collected in a pile at her feet. Eyes panicked and pitiful, she gestures to you once again for help.

Beams of light emerge sourcelessly from the ground, weaving between each and carving intricate fractals in their trails. Slag and glass and wet, stinking heat well up along the edges of the channels the lights carve. As their work is almost complete, it becomes clear that their labor was the carving of a summoning circle. Its runes are carved in the language of the lost Martian philosophers. A being is to be born here, in mere moments, from the time of one of humanity’s failed ascensions to godhood.

The song of hammers striking metal fills your minds. Each blow solidifies in you an everstronger image of an arm being forged. Your every footfall drives in sync with the pounding of the forge-hammer. A curling dome of a building can be seen nearby. You know this to be the seat of the forge. Hellsteel fingers curl above the anvil at the building’s center, shading it from the sun of Hell. Though the sound of creation rings so loud in your heads as to be overwhelming, the forge before you lays still—fires unlit, bellows inert, no metal to be forged.

Shadows on the ground creep in bizarre crescents, coiling and twisting inwardly on themselves in a disorientating display. After a while, these crescents become entirely disconnected from their source, and they begin to multiply. Soon the entire world seems to be covered in an infinite sprawl of spiraling shadows.

You happen across a series of ramshackle huts and stalls. Demons devoid of affiliation roam about, chatting, plying trades and trading goods. Oathbreakers and The Fatherless and SelfMothers chatter readily and happily here. Demonstrations of demonic meditative practice play out in a hastily cleared space. Pacts, contracts, and ad-hoc agreements are spoken, scrawled, and signed here. A sign, seemingly made by a flaming finger drawn across black steel, hangs above the entrance to this space. It reads “Lay down your arms, drink from our cup.”

Nearby stones, loose ropes, chains, and jewelry begin to float into the air. As you realize what is taking place, your feet have already become so light you are no longer tethered to the ground. Your entire convoy has unwillingly taken to the sky. The ground below you is seemingly no more. An enormous, black shadow passes over you all. You have entered the hunting grounds of the Bythic Kite.

A yawning hole in the ground rumbles and groans. Its voice calls out to you, begging to be fed. It NEEDS to be fed, it says. Terrible things are sure to happen if it goes unsated. Surely you can offer it some small morsel?

You stumble upon the unattended ruins of what was once Hell’s great glass manufactory. Workstations were abandoned in a rush. Tools scatter the floor. Shattered glass and unrefined sand crunch beneath your feet. Beautiful phantoms of creations that might have been
